journal-meta.journal-id: Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A
Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A

journal-title-group.journal-title: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

title-group.article-title: UPF1 deficiency enhances mitochondrial ROS which promotes an immunosuppressive microenvironment in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma

contrib-group.aff: aPerlmutter Cancer Center, New York University Grossman School of Medicine, New York, NY 10016
bMoores Cancer Center, University of California, San Diego, CA 92037

author-notes.corresp: 3To whom correspondence may be addressed. Email: philim01@nyulangone.org.

fn.p: Edited by Ronald DePinho, The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston, TX; received January 29, 2024; accepted July 5, 2024
1W.S., J.K.R., and C.N.-T. contributed equally to this work.

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright © 2024 the Author(s). Published by PNAS.

license.license-p: This article is distributed under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives License 4.0 (CC BY-NC-ND).

abstract.title: Significance
abstract.p: Tumor immunotherapy is an increasingly important modality in treating cancer. Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is particularly recalcitrant to immunotherapies. This is thought to be a consequence of an immunosuppressive tumor immune microenvironment. Diminished expression of the RNA helicase upstream frameshift 1 (UPF1) has been associated with poor prognosis in PDAC. Here, we show that in a mouse model of PDAC, silencing UPF1 leads to enhanced tumor growth as a consequence of tumor-intrinsic redox dysregulation. UPF1 deficiency leads to enhanced mitochondrial complex I function and increased mitochondrial reactive oxygen species that triggers the release of cytokines that recruit myeloid-derived suppressor cells. These data suggest that therapeutic targeting of mitochondrial complex I in PDAC may augment the efficacy of immunotherapy.

Upstream frameshift 1 (UPF1) is an RNA helicase involved in a number of mRNA regulatory processes including nonsense-mediated decay. Mutations in the UPF1 locus that reduce its expression have been associated with adenosquamous carcinoma of the pancreas, a particularly aggressive form of the disease. To determine the effect of Upf1 suppression in a murine model of pancreatic adenocarcinoma, we silenced with shRNA Upf1 in cells derived from an autochthonous tumor in an LSL-KrasG12D/+; Trp53R172H/+; Pdx-1Cre/+ mouse (KPC) and orthotopically implanted these cells in the pancreas of C57BL/6 mice. Tumors derived from Upf1-deficient cells were markedly larger than those derived from control cells, a difference observed only in immunocompetent mice. The immune infiltrate of Upf1-deficient tumors was enriched in myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) and depleted of CD8+ cells compared to control KPC tumors. Upf1-deficient KPC cells secreted inflammatory cytokines including G-CSF and CXCL2, known to recruit MDSCs. Cytokine secretion from Upf1-deficient KPC cells was induced by increased levels of mitochondrial reactive oxygen species (ROS), which in turn were due to an increase in complex I activity in the electron transport chain. Thus, Upf1 helicase deficiency leads to increased mitochondrial complex I activity which produces ROS that signals for cytokine release that drives immune suppression and enhanced tumor growth.

body.p: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is a leading cause of cancer-related deaths in the United States and worldwide, with a 5-y survival rate of 12% (1). PDAC is characterized by many features that pose significant challenges to its clinical management, including its indolent progression in seemingly early-stage disease and predilection for early metastasis. Additionally, in an era where immunotherapy has been employed with increasing success, PDAC has remained particularly recalcitrant to immune strategies (2). A significant advancement in the understanding of PDAC has been the recognition of the tumor microenvironment (TME) as an important feature that makes PDAC particularly resistant to therapeutic intervention (3). The PDAC TME consists of a complex collection of cells (e.g., tumor cells, cancer-associated fibroblasts, immune cells, and neurons) woven into a dense stromal environment that, in aggregate, shields tumor cells from extraneous insults, limits immune recognition, and promotes oncogenic progression. Dissecting the molecular and cellular mechanisms that allow PDAC to orchestrate an immunosuppressive TME will be critical to devising new therapeutics and improve clinical outcomes.

Among PDACs, the adenosquamous carcinoma (ASC) subtype is highly aggressive and confers a particularly poor prognosis. ASC is associated with loss-of-function mutations in several tumor suppressor genes, including TP53, KDM6A, KMT2C, and KMT2D (4, 5). The squamous epithelial characteristics of the tumor are associated with the expression of the TP63 transcription factor (6). ASC has also been associated with mutations in the RNA helicase upstream frameshift 1 (UPF1), an integral component of the complex that mediates nonsense-mediated decay (NMD) of aberrant mRNA transcripts (7). This study reported mutations in 18 of 23 cases. Interestingly, the majority of these were intronic, clustered in intron 10, and resulting in diminished expression of the WT gene product, suggesting that it is low UPF1 expression rather than mutant protein that drives the phenotype. However, the significance of UPF1 mutations in ASC has remained controversial because another group found that a significant portion of these lesions were inherited variants rather than somatic mutations acting as functional drivers of oncogenesis (8). Although its role as a driver of ASC is uncertain, the role of UPF1 in PDAC more broadly remains unexplored.

Appreciation of UPF1 mRNA regulation has grown to include an expanding variety of processes including NMD (9), staufen-mediated decay (10), and structure-mediated decay (11). Dysregulation of these pathways has been identified in many cancers, frequently through direct modulation of UPF1 expression and function. UPF1 loss can be associated with either enhanced or diminished tumor growth, depending on the context. Loss of UPF1 through a variety of mechanisms is seen in gastric cancer, hepatocellular carcinoma, lung adenocarcinoma, ovarian cancer, and gliomas and is associated with increased cellular proliferation, invasion, and migration, suggesting UPF1 functions as a tumor suppressor in these cancers (12–17). On the other hand, loss of UPF1 in microsatellite unstable colorectal cancer (CRC) inhibits cellular proliferation, whereas microsatellite stable CRC is unaffected by UPF1 deficiency (18).

In this study, we interrogate the role of Upf1 in PDAC by employing an orthotopic murine model. Depletion of Upf1 led to enhanced tumor growth but only in immunocompetent animals. Mechanistic studies revealed that Upf1 loss leads to alterations in the mitochondrial electron transport chain (ETC) resulting in the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in PDAC tumor cells, which in turn signal for the release of proinflammatory cytokines that recruit immunosuppressive myeloid cells and allow oncogenic progression. Thus, we have found that Upf1 plays an important role in mitochondrial biology in tumor cells and this cell-autonomous alteration in turn exerts a non-cell-autonomous influence on the immune TME.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Upf1 knockout in vivo is embryonic lethal (19) and most of the mutations associated with pancreatic ASC are noncoding but result in decreased expression of the helicase. Therefore, to model the human condition, we sought means to diminish but not eliminate Upf1 expression. To accomplish this in a genetic fashion, we developed genetically modified mice that conditionally express in the pancreas mir30Upf1, a microRNA that efficiently silences Upf1. We generated mice transgenic for a Tet-inducible mir30Upf1, a reverse Tet activator under the control of a lox-stop-lox (LSL) cassette and Pdx-1-Cre such that mir30Upf1 would be expressed in the pancreas of mice fed tetracycline. We intended to cross these mice with the LSL-Kras12D model of pancreatic adenocarcinoma. However, we were surprised to observe, in the absence of oncogenic Kras, marked pancreatic atrophy in mice induced to express mir30Upf1 in that organ beginning at age 21 d (SI Appendix, Fig. S1A). This precluded the use of the autochthonous model to assess the role of Upf1 suppression in PDAC. Therefore, to evaluate the influence of Upf1 suppression on pancreatic tumorigenesis, we employed RNAi to knockdown (KD) Upf1 in a mouse pancreatic cancer cell line derived from a tumor of an LSL-Kras+/G12D LSL-Tp53R172H/+ PDX1-Cre (KPC) mouse (Fig. 1A). Orthotopic implantation of Upf1 KD KPC cells into the pancreatic tail of C57BL6/J mice generated larger tumors after 21 d of growth compared to those derived from control cells (Fig. 1 B and C). We did not observe squamous metaplasia in the histological analysis of control or Upf1 deficient tumors (SI Appendix, Fig. S1B). We measured the relative rates of growth Upf1 KD KPC and control cells in 2D culture and found no difference (Fig. 1D). Thus, the increase in tumor size cannot be explained by the accelerated growth of Upf1-deficient cells.

caption.p: Silencing Upf1 increases tumor size in a murine orthotopic PDAC model. (A) Upf1 KD in KPC cells with two different shRNAs was confirmed by immunoblot. (B) Representative images of tumors harvested at day 21 after orthotopic injection of 105 shScr or shUpf1-1 KPCs into the pancreas of C57BL/6 mice. (C) Weights of explanted tumors harvested after orthotopic injection as indicated in B. ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001, by Mann–Whitney. (D) Cell proliferation of indicated KPC cell lines in 2D culture.

sec.p: Differences observed between in vivo and in vitro growth of tumor cells can result from the influence of the TME, including the immune system. Indeed, a complex TME has been well documented to contribute to carcinogenesis and tumor progression in PDAC. One characteristic of the PDAC TME is an immunosuppressive infiltrate that plays a significant role in shielding cancer cells from surveillance by the adaptive immune system and thereby facilitates tumor growth (3). To test whether Upf1 expression in tumor cells affects the immune TME, we performed flow cytometric profiling of the immune infiltrate isolated from control and Upf1 KD orthotopic tumors (Fig. 2 A–E). Upf1 KD tumors had a significantly increased number of granulocyte-like myeloid-derived suppressor cells (G-MDSC; CD45+CD11b+Ly-6GhighLy-6C+) (Fig. 2B) and a decreased number of CD8+ T cells (Fig. 2E), consistent with immunosuppression. To determine the importance of the adaptive immune system in driving the differential tumor growth, we performed orthotopic implantation of control or Upf1 KD cells into NU/J animals that lack an adaptive immune system and observed no differences in tumor sizes between control and Upf1 KD cells (Fig. 2F), confirming the central role of the immune system in differential growth. To determine whether these observations were reflected in human disease, we analyzed 13 treatment-naive human PDAC samples from a publicly available single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) analysis that we previously published (20). The average expression of UPF1 in tumor cells correlated inversely with the fraction of each sample’s myeloid compartment that consisted of MDSCs (Fig. 2G), consistent with our finding in mice. These results suggest that UPF1 expression in tumor cells alters the immune microenvironment.

caption.p: Upf1-deficient tumors manifest an immunosuppresive TME, lose their growth advantage in nude mice, and recapitulate the relationship between the immune TME and UPF1 expression in human disease. (A) Percentage of CD45+ cells in dissociated tumors 21 d after orthotopic injection. (B–E) Percentage of CD45+ cells characterized by flow cytometry (FC) as B, G-MDSCs (CD45+CD11b+Ly-6GhighLy-6C+), C, M-MDSCs (CD45+CD11b+Ly-6GlowLy-6C+), D, CD4+ cells, E, CD8+ cells. ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001, by Mann–Whitney. (F) Weight of tumors harvested at day 21 after orthotopic injection of 105 shScr or shUpf1-1 KPCs into the pancreas of Nude mice. (G) scRNA-Seq transcriptomic analysis of treatment-naive human primary pancreatic adenocarcinomas plotting percent of myeloid cells that demonstrate an MDSC transcriptomic signature as a function of average tumor cell UPF1 expression. Each point represents one sample. The correlation coefficient and significance are shown, which indicate an inverse correlation. Gray area depicts the 95% confidence region.

sec.p: One mechanism by which tumor cells modulate the immune microenvironment is by the production of soluble factors such as cytokines that are able to both recruit immune cells and reprogram their behavior. We measured the amounts of immunomodulatory cytokines and chemokines in the cell culture supernatants of control and Upf1 KD KPC cells via Luminex Multiplex assays and found that Upf1 KD cells secrete elevated amounts of multiple proinflammatory cytokines and chemokines (Dataset S1). Among these, Upf1 KD led to elevated secretion of G-CSF and CXCL2 (Fig. 3A), both of which have been previously shown to play a role in orchestrating the immunosuppressive milieu of PDAC (21). This result was confirmed by ELISA (SI Appendix, Fig. S2 A and B). Similar results were obtained with an independent pancreatic KPC cell line (SI Appendix, Fig. S2C). To determine whether this phenomenon was specific to KPC cells derived from pancreatic tumors, we also tested a cell line derived from a KRAS-driven, p53-deficient mouse lung adenocarcinoma and found that silencing UPF1 increased G-CSF but not CXCL2 secretion (SI Appendix, Fig. S2 D and E), demonstrating tumor-type differences in response to UPF1 deficiency. We confirmed elevated expression of cytokines in pancreatic tumor-derived KPC cells by measuring mRNA transcripts by RNA-Seq (Fig. 3B and Dataset S2) performed on cDNA libraries generated from control and Upf1 KD KPC cells grown in vitro as well as by RT-qPCR (Fig. 3C). To determine whether the increased expression of cytokines was due to loss of Upf1 expression, we stably reintroduced a hairpin-resistant Upf1 cDNA in control and Upf1 KD lines and measured the expression of G-CSF in the supernatant by ELISA. Re-expression of UPF1 in the KD line reduced G-CSF expression to levels observed in the control cell line (Fig. 3D). Interestingly, ectopic expression of Upf1 in control cells reduced the G-CSF expression below baseline, further demonstrating that Upf1 levels are inversely correlated with the expression of this proinflammatory cytokine. Forced expression of UPF1 also partially reversed the increased CXCL2 secretion observed when endogenous UPF1 was silenced (SI Appendix, Fig. S2F). Importantly, we observed an inverse correlation of CXCL2 expression with UPF1 levels (SI Appendix, Fig. S2G) in the 13 human pancreatic cancer samples that showed the same inverse correlation with MDSC cells (Fig. 2G).

caption.p: Upf1 deficiency is associated with increased secretion of inflammatory cytokines and bulk RNA-seq implicates associated mitochondrial dysfunction. (A) Representative cytokines (G-CSF/Csf3, CXCL2/MIP-2, GM-CSF/Csf2, CCL2/MCP-1) measured by Multiplex Luminex assays in the conditioned media of shScr and shUpf1 KPCs after 3 d in culture. (B) mRNA levels of indicated cytokines in KPCs were measured by RNAseq. The fold change in expression in shUpf1 relative to shScr cells is plotted. (C) mRNA levels of Csf2, Csf3, and Lif were measured by RT-PCR in shScr versus shUpf1 cells. (D) The increase in G-CSF in the conditioned media of shUpf1 cells measured by ELISA is abrogated by re-expression of Upf1. Immunoblot in the Upper panel shows silencing of Upf1 and re-expression of a myc-tagged Upf1. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001, by Mann–Whitney. (E and F) Significantly different (FDR < 0.05) MSigDB GO Biological Process and MSigDb GO Cellular Compartment gene sets in shUpf1 versus shScr KPC cell-based genes that were differentially expressed in RNA-Seq analysis.

sec.p: One safeguard against inappropriate expression of proinflammatory cytokines takes place posttranscriptionally whereby cytokine mRNAs that have stem-loop structures in their 3′ UTRs are targeted for degradation by the RNase Regnase-1, which cooperates with UPF1 to effect RNA decay (22). Accordingly, we tested the role of Regnase-1 and UPF1 in G-CSF mRNA stability. We generated independent KPC KD cell lines for Regnase I (Zc3h12a). As expected, Zc3h12a knockout cells demonstrate increased expression of G-CSF mRNA and protein (SI Appendix, Fig. S3 A and B). We performed actinomycin D mRNA stability assays on control, Upf1 KD, and Zc3h12a KD KPC cells and found that while Zc3h12a deficiency results in Csf3 (G-CSF) and Cxcl2 mRNA instability, Upf1 deficiency does not (SI Appendix, Fig. S3 C and D). These results suggest that the increase in Csf3 (G-CSF) and Cxcl2 mRNA seen in Upf1 KD cells is not due to increased stability of the mRNA but rather to increases in transcriptional activity.

To identify the mechanism(s) by which Upf1 drives expression of immunomodulatory cytokines, we performed pathway analysis on RNA-seq data generated from cDNA libraries of Upf1 KD and control cells (Dataset S2). These analyses predicted association of UPF1 deficiency with granulocyte chemotaxis and migration, increased cytokine production, and modulation of the innate immune response (Fig. 3E), in accord with the immune profiling seen in orthotopic tumors. These analyses also revealed significant differences in mitochondrial transcripts between Upf1 KD and control cells with a specific defect in the mitochondrial ETC (Fig. 3F).

In order to validate the RNA-seq data at the protein level, we performed immunoblot analysis of a number of mitochondrial proteins (SI Appendix, Fig. S4A). The amount of HSP60 and TOM70 detected per cell did not differ between control and Upf1-deficient cells, suggesting that mitochondrial mass was unaffected. This result was confirmed using MitoTracker Green (SI Appendix, Fig. S4B). In contrast, we found a significant decrease in the expression of various subunits of the oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) system including SDHB (complex II), UQCRC2 (complex III), and ATP5B (ATP synthase or complex V) in Upf1 KD cells compared to controls (SI Appendix, Fig. S4A). Interestingly, no significant differences were found in the expression of proteins of complexes I and IV. Overall, these results demonstrate that Upf1 is required for normal expression of a number of mitochondrial proteins involved in OXPHOS. To investigate whether the changes in protein expression affect mitochondrial function, we explored mitochondrial respiration using Seahorse analysis of Upf1 KD and control cells (SI Appendix, Fig. S4C). Surprisingly, we found no differences in mitochondrial respiration other than a modest increase in the maximum respiration of Upf1 KD cells compared to the control (SI Appendix, Fig. S4C). Given that the loss of Upf1 does not dramatically affect the protein expression of the entire OXPHOS system, we performed a more comprehensive analysis of ETC function by characterizing the activity of the single complexes. We measured the enzymatic activities of complexes I to IV in isolated mitochondria from control and Upf1 KD cells (Fig. 4A) and found significantly increased activity of complex I in Upf1 KD cells, whereas the activities of complexes II to IV were preserved (Fig. 4A).

caption.p: The effect of Upf1 deficiency on the activities of ETC complexes I to IV, mitochondrial ROS, and phosphorylation and assembly of complex I. (A) Complex I, II, III, and IV activities measured in mitochondria isolated from shScr and shUpf1 KPC cells. (B and C) Mitochondrial ROS production measured with MitoSOX relative to the total mitochondrial mass measured with MitoTracker Green. Results are shown as arbitrary units of fluorescence. (B) Upf1 expression was stably rescued with a cDNA resistant to the short harpin (Upf1 cDNA) or an empty vector as a control (EV). (C) Cells were treated with the mitochondrial ROS scavenger MitoTEMPO (3 d, 1 μM) or the vehicle. (D) G-CSF levels measured by ELISA in cells with or without treatment with MitoTEMPO (3 d, 1 μM) or the vehicle. Results are normalized to the shScr. (A–D) Bars indicate mean ± SEM. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, and ***P < 0.001 when compared by one-way ANOVA. (E) Immunocapture of Complex I from mitochondria isolated from shScr or shUpf1 cells analyzed by antiphosphoserine/threonine immunoblot. NDUFA6 immunoblot and a Coomassie-stained gel are shown as loading controls. (F) Representative Blue Native (BN) gels of mitochondrial membrane proteins blotted with the antibodies to the indicated subunits of OXPHOS complexes. The blots reveal complex I and In + IIIn (NDUFS1), complex III2 and In + IIIn (UQCRC2), and complex IV (COX5A). A VDAC immunoblot is shown as loading control.

sec.p: Mitochondria are one of the main sources of cellular ROS and complex I of the ETC is a primary site of ROS production (23). Complex-I-generated ROS is critical for redox signaling from the mitochondria to the rest of the cell (24). One consequence of this signaling is the generation of proinflammatory soluble mediators (25, 26). We therefore determined the amount of mitochondrial and total ROS in control versus Upf1-deficient cells. Compared to control cells, Upf1 KD cells produce significantly higher amounts of both mitochondrial (mtROS) and total cellular ROS (Fig. 4B and SI Appendix, Fig. S4D). Rescue of Upf1 KD with a hairpin-resistant Upf1 cDNA restored the ROS to the levels measured in control cells (Fig. 4B), confirming a specific role of Upf1 in promoting ROS production.

mtROS signaling can stimulate the production of proinflammatory cytokines (27). To test the role of mtROS in G-CSF production, we employed a mitochondrially targeted antioxidant, MitoTEMPO. Interestingly, MitoTEMPO failed to decrease mtROS levels of control cells, suggesting very low basal levels of mtROS (Fig. 4C). However, after treatment with MitoTEMPO, the excess in mtROS levels shown in Upf1 KD cells was returned to the baseline levels of observed control cells (Fig. 4C). The same treatment reduced secreted G-CSF in Upf1 KD cell cultures back to the level of control cells (Fig. 4D), demonstrating the role of complex I-generated mtROS in inducing G-CSF levels in Upf1 deficient cells.

Complex I activity can be modulated by expression levels of its component proteins or through posttranslational modifications (e.g., phosphorylation) of those components (28). Analysis of NDUFB8, NDUFS1, NDUFS3, and NDUFS4, four subunits of complex I, showed no difference in expression in Upf1 deficient cells (SI Appendix, Fig. S4A). In contrast, complex I proteins immunocaptured from mitochondria isolated from Upf1 KD cells revealed increased phosphorylation relative to those immunocaptured from the mitochondria of control cells (Fig. 4E). Complex I assembly as a single complex or as a supercomplex can be analyzed by BN gel electrophoresis (29). This method of analysis applied to the extracts of mitochondria isolated from control and Upf1 KD cells revealed an increase in Upf1-deficient cells in complex I both as complex I itself and in supercomplexes containing complex III (Fig. 4F), consistent with increased complex I activity. Collectively, these data suggest that loss of Upf1 in KPC cells leads to increased phosphorylation of subunits and assembly of complexes, and thus activity of complex I. This increased activity results in a modest increase in ROS production that initiates mitohormetic signaling (30) that results in the production of soluble factors such as immunomodulatory cytokines and chemokines that suppress the adaptive immune response to the tumor.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The association of UPF1 mutations with an aggressive form of PDAC (7) drew our attention to this RNA helicase. We found that silencing Upf1 in murine KRAS and p53 mutant PDAC cells used to generate an orthotopic tumors resulted in markedly enlarged tumors. However, we found no evidence of squamous metaplasia suggesting that Upf1 deficiency in this mouse model is not sufficient to recapitulate human ASC. The increase in tumor size required an intact adaptive immune system. This was somewhat surprising because silencing Upf1 is predicted to inhibit NMD and thereby stabilize aberrant transcripts generating neoantigens and enhancing antitumor immunity (31). Potential for neoantigen generation notwithstanding, the result could be explained by our observation of an increase in immunosuppressive myeloid cells in Upf1-deficient tumors. Infiltration of G-MDSCs into tumors is generally driven by proinflammatory cytokines (21) and indeed we found that silencing Upf1 in KPC cells increased secretion of a number of such mediators, including G-CSF and CXCL2 that are known to attract G-MDSCs (21). This raised the question of how the Upf1 RNA helicase might suppress the expression of G-CSF and CXCL2 in tumor cells. One parsimonious explanation would be through the action of regnase-1, an RNase that cooperates with Upf1 to keep inflammatory cytokine production in check by degrading their mRNAs (22). However, we ruled out this mechanism by showing that while G-CSF mRNA stability was enhanced by silencing regnase-1, silencing Upf1 had no effect.

We therefore explored other mechanisms by taking the unbiased approach of comparative transcriptomics. We found that among the pathways most perturbed in Upf1-deficient cells was mitochondrial electron transport. Because the mitochondrial ETC is responsible for generating cellular ROS, which is known to induce the secretion of inflammatory cytokines, we explored the relationship between Upf1 and the ETC. An effective mitochondrial electron transfer via the ETC culminating in ADP phosphorylation to generate ATP via OXPHOS is among the most tightly regulated processes within mitochondria (32). The OXPHOS system can be modulated at different levels, including posttranslational modifications (33) and protein complex assembly (29), which are crucial to ensure the efficiency of the system. Pathway analysis on RNA-seq data revealed a decrease in mitochondrial transcripts after loss of Upf1. While we validated the decrease at the protein level of a number of OXPHOS subunits, Seahorse analysis failed to show significant mitochondrial respiratory defects and the enzymatic activity of the individual ETC complexes revealed an increase in complex I activity. We also observed increased complex I protein phosphorylation and complex assembly, consistent with the enhanced activity. We can speculate that the upregulation of complex I formation and activity is a compensatory mechanism in response to the decrease in other OXPHOS subunits, explaining the observed maintenance of mitochondrial respiration.

Complex I is a substantial source of ROS production by mitochondria (23) and the likely site of ROS production in Upf1-deficient cells. Our data support a mechanism for enhanced complex I activity that involves both the phosphorylation and content and assembly of complex I (28, 34, 35). Interestingly, both the loss of complex I and increased activity of the complex can generate ROS. Pathological complex I deficiency leads to a very large boost in ROS production, which results in cell death (23). In contrast, there is always some electron leak from complex I that generates low levels of ROS that increase as complex I activity increases (36). Our data suggest that the loss of Upf1 leads to modest ROS production consistent with the electron leak caused by the activity of the complex. Rather than inducing cell death, this low-level ROS production can signal to survival pathways as part of a process known as mitohormesis, an important phenomenon in normal physiology and a critical modulator of various disease processes (30).

We found an increase in mtROS production that can be rescued by the reintroduction of Upf1 and quenched by the addition of the mitochondrial-targeted antioxidant MitoTEMPO. mtROS signaling can stimulate the production of proinflammatory cytokines (25–27). Indeed, we observed that mtROS quenching decreased G-CSF production in Upf1-deficient cells. Interestingly, mtROS levels in control KPC cells could not be lowered by MitoTEMPO treatment, suggesting low basal levels of mtROS in these cells. Therefore, we consider that any increase in mtROS within these cells should be sufficient to stimulate signaling pathways that lead to the production of soluble factors such as immunomodulatory cytokines and chemokines that suppress the adaptive immune response to the tumor allowing tumor growth.

How UPF1, an RNA helicase, regulates mitochondrial ETC activity remains to be determined. Interestingly, yeast UPF1 was originally isolated as a multicopy suppressor of mitochondrial splicing deficiency, and its deletion leads to an impairment in respiratory-dependent growth (37). The same authors later demonstrated that overexpression of Upf1p compensates for mitochondrial splicing deficiency independently from its function in NMD in yeasts (38). Thus, UPF1 may have a role in processing transcripts encoding ETC components.

Mitochondria, ROS, and complex I are considered attractive therapeutic targets in cancer (39–41). Although antioxidants are a logical therapeutic approach, paradoxically they have been shown to accelerate the progression of lung cancer in mice (42). Nevertheless, OXPHOS rates are highly heterogeneous among PDAC tumors, and high OXPHOS PDAC tumors are enriched in mitochondrial respiratory complex I, suggesting that PDAC patients with high complex I activity might benefit from therapies that include inhibiting this complex (43). Relatively low expression levels of UPF1 might serve as a biomarker for those tumors most likely to respond to complex I inhibition.

sec.title: Genetically Engineered Mouse.
sec.p: We generated a transgenic mouse by inserting a tetracycline-inducible mir30 microRNA targeting Upf1 (TRE-mir30Upf1) and a reverse Tet activator (rTA) under the control of a lox-stop-lox (LSL) cassette (CAG-LSL-rTA-IRES-mKate2) into the Rosa26 locus of a C57BL/6 mouse. When crossed with Pdx-1-Cre mice, mir30Upf1 is expressed exclusively in the pancreas of mice upon feeding with doxycycline.

sec.title: Cell Culture, Plasmids, Transfection, and Virus Infection.
sec.p: Murine 4662 or 1203 KPC cells were derived from a pancreatic tumor isolated from a fully backcrossed C57BL/6 LSL-KrasG12D/+; Trp53R172H/+; Pdx-1Cre/+(KPC) mouse, recapitulating the desmoplastic feature of autochthonous PDAC (44). KPC cells were cultured in DMEM containing 10% fetal bovine serum. Validated LKO.1 based UPF1 shRNAs, UPF1 expression plasmids, the lentivirus production, and target cell infection were previously described (45).

sec.title: Orthotopic Injection and Tissue Harvest.
sec.p: For orthotopic experiments, 8- to 10-wk-old male C57BL/6 (000664) and nude mice (NU/J 002019) were purchased from Jackson Labs (Bar Harbor, ME) and housed in a mouse facility at New York University Langone Medical Center. Briefly, KPC cells with shScr or shUpf1 were suspended in PBS with 50% Matrigel (BD Biosciences), and 1 × 105 cells were injected into the pancreas via laparotomy. Mice were killed on day 21 and tumors were then harvested and weighed. All animal studies were approved by and were conducted in accordance with the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee at NYU Grossman School of Medicine policies.

sec.title: FC.
sec.p: Single-cell suspensions of PDA tumors were prepared for flow cytometry (FC) as described previously (46). Briefly, tumors isolated from the recipient mice were placed in cold PBS with 1% FBS with 1 mg/mL Collagenase IV (Worthington Biochemical), 1 mg/mL Trypsin inhibitor (EMD Millipore), and 2U/ml DNase I (Promega), and minced with scissors to sub-millimeter pieces. Tissues were incubated at 37 °C for 20 min under gentle shaking every 5 min and passed through a 70-µm cell strainer (Falcon) and then centrifuged at 350×g for 7 min. Cell pellets were washed with cold PBS with 1% FBS and centrifuged. Then, pellets were resuspended in 4 mL of 40% OptiPrep (D1556, Sigma) and carefully layered the cell mix on the top of 4 mL of RPMI buffer in a 15 mL Falcon tube. Samples were centrifuged at 4 °C at 700×g for 20 min without using brake. The top layer containing mononuclear immune cells was transferred to a new 15-mL Falcon tube and 10 mL PBS with 1% FBS was added and then centrifuged at 350×g for 7 min. Cell pellets were resuspended in PBS and stained with Zombie Yellow Fixable viability kit (Biolegend). After wash, cell pellets were resuspended in FACS buffer with an anti-CD16/CD32 mAb (eBioscience) to block FcγRIII/II. After blocking, cells were labeled by incubating 1 × 106 cells with fluorescently conjugated mAbs directed against mouse CD45, CD4, CD8, CD11b, Gr-1, Ly-6G, Ly-6C, CD11c, and MHCII (Biolegend). FC was performed on LSR-II or FACSymphony (BD Biosciences). Data were analyzed using FLowJo.

sec.title: Luminex Mutiplex Assay.
sec.p: First, 1.5 million of shScr or shUpf1KPC cells were seeded in 10-cm plates with 10 mL of growth medium, and the supernatants were collected 72 h later. Cytokine levels in the supernatant were measured using mouse Cytokine/Chemokine 32-plex Assay (MILLIPLEX, Millipore) on a Luminex® SD system (Luminex). Concentrations (pg/ml) of each protein were derived from 5-parameter curve fitting models according to the manufacturer’s instructions.

sec.title: Bulk RNA Sequencing and Analysis.
sec.p: RNA was extracted from biological triplicates of Upf1 KD and control KPC cells with an RNeasy Mini kit (QIAGEN) and analyzed by Bioanalyzer for quality control and screened for RNA integrity by RIN. cDNA libraries were generated from the samples by automated stranded RNA-Seq library prep with poly A selection and sequenced by an Illumina NovaSeq 6000 using an S2 100 cycle v 1.5 flow cell at a depth of 30 million reads per sample. Raw reads were analyzed via the automated workflow pipeline Seq-N-Slide (https://igordot.github.io/sns/). Briefly, raw reads were fed into “rna-star” module of Seq-N-Slide which employs Trimmomatic for adaptor trimming and low-quality base removal, STAR for alignment to reference genomes (mm10), fastq_screen for contaminant detection, Picard for base distribution and 5′/3′ biases, and featureCounts to generate genes-samples count matrices. Count matrices were then analyzed via the “rna-star-groups-dge” module, which employs DESeq2 to determine differential gene expression, perform gene set enrichment analyses, and generate PCA, volcano, and heatmap plots.

sec.title: scRNA-seq Analysis.
sec.p: The data used for the single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) analysis of human pancreatic adenocarcinomas were taken from our published dataset (20) and is publicly available on GEO with accession number GSE205013. Briefly, the dataset consists of 27 patient samples of which 13 were pancreatic primary tumors collected prior to treatment; only those 13 samples were used in the present analysis. The epithelial cell cluster was identified based on the expression of KRT19 and other standard markers and was filtered to just malignant epithelial cells using InferCNV version 1.8.1 (47). Other relevant compartments identified include the myeloid compartment (primary markers CD68, PTPRC) and its subcompartment of MDSCs (primary markers S100A8, S100A9, and S100A12). Further analyses were performed as described (48) using scooter (49).

sec.title: RT-PCR.
sec.p: Cell pellets were collected and subjected to total RNA extraction using an RNeasy Plus Mini kit (QIAGEN) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. cDNA was generated from 1 μg RNA utilizing iScript cDNA Synthesis kit (1708891, Bio-Rad). cDNA was amplified with SsoAdvanced Universal SYBR Green Supermix (Bio-Rad, Cat1725270) using gene-specific primers optimized for qPCR that were ordered from IDT (sequences below). Amplification and detection were performed using a Bio-Rad CFX96 Touch Real-Time PCR Detection System. RT-qPCR data were normalized to Tbp and presented as fold changes of gene expression in the test sample compared to the control.

Tbp: 5′-TGTATCTACCTGAATCTTGGC-3′ 5′-CCAGAACTGAAAATCAACGAG-3′ Actb: 5′-GATTACTGCTCTGGCTCCTAG-3′ 5′-GACTCATCGTACTCCTGCTTG-3′ Csf2: 5′-GTCTCTAAC GAGTTCCTTCA-3′ 5′-CCTTGAGTTTGGTGAAATTGCC-3′ Csf3; 5′-GACTTGCITCAG CGGATGT-3′ 5′-TGGAAGGCAGAAGTGAAGG-3′ Lif: 5′-GC AACCTCATGAACCAGATCA-3′ 5′-GCACATAGCTTTTCCACGTTG-3′ Cxcl2: 5′-CAGA AGTCATAGCCACCTCAAG-3′ 5′-CTTTCCAGGTCAGTTAGCCTT-3′.

sec.title: RNA Stability Assay.
sec.p: iKPC cells derived from iKPC mice (50) maintained in medium containing 1 μg/mL doxycycline were treated with Actinomycin D (A9415, Sigma-Aldrich) at 10 μg/mL over 6 h prior to lysis and RNA isolation with RNeasy Plus Mini kit (QIAGEN). cDNA was generated from 1 μg of RNA utilizing iScript cDNA Synthesis kit (1708891, Bio-Rad). Csf3, Cxcl2, and Tbp-specific primers optimized for qPCR quantification were purchased from IDT (sequences shown above). qPCR reactions were carried out utilizing SsoAdvanced Universal SYBR Green Supermix (1725271, Bio-Rad) and measured utilizing a Bio-Rad CFX96 Touch Real-Time PCR Detection System. mRNA decay was calculated as a percentage of intial transcipts via the following assumption:Fraction of Transcript Remaining=0.5(CqTimepoint-CqInitial Timepoint).

sec.title: Protein Extraction and Western Blot Analysis.
sec.p: Cells were lysed in lysis buffer (50 mM Tris, pH 7.5, 150 mM NaCl, 10 mM NaF, 1 mM EDTA, and 1% NP40) supplemented with 1×protease inhibitor tablet (Roche) and 1×PhosStop tablet (Sigma-Aldrich). Lysates were clarified by centrifugation at 11,000×g for 15 min. The resulting supernatants were fractionated on SDS-PAGE and transferred onto polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) or nitrocellulose membranes for immunoblot analysis. Protein concentrations were determined using Bradford reagent (Bio-Rad protein assay). The following antibodies were used for western blotting: anti-UPF1 (9435, Cell Signaling), anti-Myc (2276, Cell Signaling), anti-Vinculin (13901, Cell Signaling), anti-Phospho-Ser/Thr (9631S, Cell Signaling), anti-NDUFA6 (NBP2-93398, Novus Biologicals), anti-HSP60 (ab46798, Abcam), anti-TOM70 (14528-1-AP, Proteintech), anti-NDUFB8 (ab110242, Abcam), anti-NDUFS1 (ABN302, Millipore Sigma), anti-NDUFS3 (ab110246, Abcam), anti-NDUFS4 (GTX105662, GeneTex), anti-SDHB (ab14714, Abcam), anti-SDHA (ab14715, Abcam), anti-UQCRC2 (ab14745, Abcam), anti-MTCO1 (459600, Thermo Fisher Scientific), anti-COX5A (ab180129, Abcam), anti-ATP5B (ab14730, Abcam), and anti-VDAC (4866, Cell Signaling).

sec.title: Isolation of Mitochondria.
sec.p: Cells were homogenized in a glass-Teflon homogenizer with seven volumes of hypotonic buffer (83 mM sucrose, 10 mM MOPS pH 7.2). After homogenization, the same volume of hypertonic buffer (250 mM sucrose, 30 mM MOPS pH 7.2) was added and nuclei and unbroken cells were removed by centrifugation at 1,000×g. Mitochondria were obtained by centrifugation at 12,000×g and washed in buffer A (320 mM sucrose, 1 mM ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), 10 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.4) (34).

sec.title: Mitochondrial Respiration.
sec.p: Mitochondrial respiratory parameters of 30,000 Upf1 KD and control KPC cells were determined in XFe96 Seahorse flux analyzer (Agilent Technologies) using 10 mM glucose, 1 mM pyruvate, and 2 mM glutamine. To assess OSR, maximum respiration, and non-mitochondrial-dependent oxygen consumption, respectively, 2 μM oligomycin (OL), 2 μM of carbonyl cyanide-4-(trifluoromethoxy)phenylhydrazone (FCCP), and 1 μM rotenone plus 1 μM antimycin were used (34).

sec.title: Mitochondrial Enzyme Activities and BN Electrophoresis.
sec.p: Isolated mitochondria from Upf1 KD and control KPC cells were used for the spectrophotometric determination of complexes I (ab109721), II (ab109908), III (ab287844), and IV (ab109911) following the manufacturer’s instructions. For BN gels, mitochondrial pellets were suspended in 50 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.0 containing 1M 6-aminohexanoic acid at a final concentration of 10 mg mL−1. The membranes were solubilized by the addition of 10% digitonin (4:1 digitonin/mitochondrial protein). Then, 5% Serva Blue G dye in 1M 6-aminohexanoic acid was added to the solubilized membranes. Native PAGE™ Novex® 3 to 12% Bis-Tris Protein Gels (Life Technologies) were loaded with 30 μg of mitochondrial protein. After fractionation, the gels were electroblotted onto PVDF membranes. Membranes were further processed for immunoblotting (34).

sec.title: Mitochondrial Mass and ROS Production.
sec.p: Mitochondrial mass was measured in Upf1 KD and control KPC cells using MitoTracker Green (M7514, Thermo Fisher Scientific). Then, 25,000 cells per well were seeded in a black, clear bottom 96-well plate in DMEM. The next day, the medium was removed, and cells were incubated in 150 μL of 50 nM MitoTracker Green in prewarmed DMEM for 30 min. The mitochondrial production of ROS in Upf1 KD and control KPC cells was monitored by changes in fluorescence signal using MitoSOX Red (M36008, Thermo Fisher Scientific) (51). Then, 25,000 cells per well were seeded in a black, clear bottom 96-well plate in DMEM. Next day, the medium was removed, and cells were washed twice with PBS 1X and incubated in 150 μL of 5 μM MitoSOX in prewarmed recording media (DMEM no phenol red with HEPES) for 30 min. Then, 2 μM rotenone was used as an internal positive control. The intracellular production of hydrogen peroxide was monitored by changes in fluorescence signal using the cell-permeant 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (H2DCFDA) (D399, Thermo Fisher Scientific). Further, 25,000 cells per well were seeded in a black, cleat bottom 96-well plate in DMEM. Next day, the medium was removed, and cells were washed twice with PBS 1X and incubated in 150 μL of 5 μM H2DCFDA in prewarmed recording media (HBSS) for 30 min. Changes in fluorescence were measured in a EnSpire® Multimode Plate Reader (Perkin Elmer) plate reader. Then, 100 μM H202 was used as an internal positive control.

sec.title: Immunocapture Assay.
sec.p: Respiratory Complex I was immunopurified from isolated mitochondria of Upf1 KD and control KPC cells using a commercial kit (ab109711, Abcam) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. The beads were washed twice before complexes were eluted and fractionated on SDS-PAGE. After fractionation, the gels were electroblotted onto PVDF membranes. Membranes were further processed for immunoblotting.

ack.p: This research was supported by grants from the NIH (R01CA211687 to L.B.G. and M.R.P.; R35CA253178 to M.R.P.; F31CA261120 to J.K.R.) and the Ramón Areces Foundation (to C.N.-T.).

sec.title: Author contributions
sec.p: W.S., J.K.R., K.-k.W., D.M.S., L.B.G., and M.R.P. designed research; W.S., J.K.R., C.N.-T., and C.B. performed research; T.C. and L.B.G. contributed new reagents/analytic tools; W.S., J.K.R., C.N.-T., E.K., K.-k.W., D.M.S., and M.R.P. analyzed data; and W.S., J.K.R., C.N.-T., and M.R.P. wrote the paper.

sec.title: Competing interests
sec.p: The authors declare no competing interest.

fn.p: This article is a PNAS Direct Submission.

sec.title: Data, Materials, and Software Availability
sec.p: Data has been deposited in the NCBI SRA database through the accession number: PRJNA1138276 (52). These data are also included in the supporting information.
